### Wilma Without a W — 2025-11-05T15:29:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ilmas-optimal-journal.30804/post-362200>

In other news, things are going well for us here in El Salvador. DH likes it here and interestingly enough I'm the one that's suffering more from the heat than he is. Of course he does spend 20 hours out of the day sleeping, inside, so that could account for it.

I tried purchasing some hammocks but come to find out they're too long for the hooks that are on the house. And then I thought of what might happen should he have a seizure while in one of those hammocks and called it a blessing that they didn't work.

Now I need to get him a cot or something for outside here so he can snooze away outdoors.

He has had one seizure since being here, about 2-3 weeks after we got here.

And as for me, I'm slowly working on my tan. This is already the darkest I've ever been, but I'm going for broke.

I highly recommend the Costa del Sol area for anyone who likes peace and quiet and a beautiful beach. It's a bit of a PIA to get into San Salvador but it's doable if you time your trip to avoid the traffic.

### Wilma Without a W — 2025-12-01T17:43:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ilmas-optimal-journal.30804/post-363446>

I will be 60 in 1 week.

I remember my 50th birthday, my 40th birthday, but not my 30th or 20th or much less 10th.

The last thing I ever imagined, however, was that I would be turning 60 in El Salvador.

I've lived in Paraguay and also Argentina, and so the idea of living in a foreign country wasn't...foreign...
it's just that it was never conceived as an option.

And you know what's interesting about this place? It reminds me of how things were in the US during my childhood. I see children routinely riding in the back of a pickup truck with or without their parents. I see children (and adults) playing soccer outside, on the beach, every night. I see kids hustling their equivalent of a lemonade stand, selling trinkets to tourists on the beach. I see freedom.

Not that some of the things I see are particularly safe, however. I've cringed more than once.

And the dogs. The poor dogs.

But I'm digressing.

60\. Maybe it's a natural part of turning a zero and leaving the nines behind to become more contemplative.

So now I'm going to go contemplate while I gather some shells on the beach.

### Wilma Without a W — 2026-03-12T19:14:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ilmas-optimal-journal.30804/post-368649>

I am in a quandary.

DH's level of functioning has been slowly declining. Looking back, it began before we left New Hampshire, but the decline peaked in early January after we returned from a 10 day visit to the states. The purpose of that visit was to have the VNS adjusted with a new to us neurologist at the University of Miami.

A couple of days after our return to ES, DH fell and hit his face. Later that day he displayed an increase in aphasia, confusion, poor balance, etc. My assumption was that those symptoms were a result of his fall. In retrospect I see that he fell because he already had hydrocephalus.

I obtained an order for a CT and took him to the capital immediately to get it done. The CT confirmed "mild" hydrocephalus based on the size of one enlarged ventricle, and also sinus congestion.

(BTW, DH has a shunt so he shouldn't have any hydrocephalus if it's working properly. I started to become concerned that his shunt might be malfunctioning after the CT scan. I was told when they implanted the shunt 9 years ago that it had a functional life of about 10 years. At the time they told me to look out for headache as a sign that it might be malfunctioning, but DH has never had any headaches. And yet here we are.)

We obtained a consult with a local neurologist who told us simply to wait it out and to keep him as low stim as possible. He conjectured the fall had caused the hydrocephalus and that the sinus congestion might have aggravated it, but that he would consult with a neurosurgeon and let us know what the next steps would be.

It took close to a month to obtain the consult with the neurosurgeon, and when we went to see her, she checked the shunt and stated that it seemed to be working properly as far as that goes. She suggested performing a spinal tap to determine how much intra cranial pressure he may still have because she conjectured that the amount of CSF was greater than his (fixed) shunt could cope with.

We agreed with that plan and she performed the spinal tap a few days later.

DH's ICP was found to be 30 mmHg (she said 8-12 is normal), so she removed enough fluid to bring him down to 10. She stated that if it were her father she would schedule him for surgery to replace the fixed shunt with an adjustable one, STAT.

3 days after the spinal tap DH experiences a seizure here at home. The last seizure he had had was back in early October of last year. I don't know if it's possible that the sudden decrease in ICP triggered the seizure. Fortunately, he recovered from that seizure fairly quickly and has not had another one.

Thing is, DH has a history of status epilepticus. Bad.

I've been advised by physicians who practice both in El Salvador and in the states to take him to the states to have the shunt replaced in case of complications.

I reached out to the University of Miami and obtained a referral to neurosurgery. They offered him an intake appointment for next week, but stated that it would likely be at least two or three weeks after that before any surgery would be scheduled.

I also reached out to Dartmouth Hitchcock medical center in NH which is where the original shunt was placed, and am waiting to hear back from them as to when they might offer an appointment. My guess is that they are going to be unable to offer an appointment for surgery any sooner than a month from now.

The neurosurgeon here in ES says she can perform the procedure within a few days time.

My hope was that by her removing the CSF and bringing DH back to normal pressure, that it would gain me some time.

But last night DH was incontinent, which is not at all a typical thing, and here and there the aphasia is showing up again, and I'm not sure how much time I have. I'm afraid his ICP might be going up again.

Because my limited understanding is that the risks of any consequences or complications increase with higher ICP, and my greatest fear is that the shunt replacement might trigger more seizure activity and potentially send him into status.

So the quandary is: do I schedule him for the procedure here in ES while his ICP is still presumably lower than it would be a month from now, and risk him going into status here...

...Or do I put him in an airplane, subject him to all of the exposure that a major US city with towers EVERYWHERE offers, while we wait for the surgery to get scheduled, with his ICP increasing all the while...only to potentially have a higher risk of him going into status in the US.

And such is my quandary.

I should add that the replacement shunt available here is the STRATA MR 2, which is likely to be the same shunt he would get in the US. I've also been assured that the quality of care here is comparable. Language isn't an issue for me, because I'm fluent in Spanish and I assume that I would stay there at the hospital with him during his recovery, and be able to alert the staff if I see something wrong. Because he doesn't speak Spanish so they wouldn't be able to assess him unless I'm there to translate.

### Jack Kruse — 2026-03-17T22:15:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ilmas-optimal-journal.30804/post-369123>

Hydrocephalus is a salt wasting disease. So BP should rise.
**Hydrocephalus-associated hyponatremia** is increasingly recognized as a clinical state where **Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW)** plays a significant role. In my Lagrangian framework, hydrocephalus is the ultimate "Massive Clog" in the brain's ventricular "exhaust pipes," leading to a systemic salt-waste to manage the isotopic burden.

They want me to give him Diovan 160mg qAM.

I'll also increase his salt intake... presumably until the BP normalizes? He's already on 3/4 tsp/liter.

High BP is due to higher blood viscosoty due to deuterium drag pre, intra, and post op due to the lack of deuterium exhaust. Not even his vagal nerve stimulation can overcome a seal shut exhaust and this changes the refraction and viscosity of his blood.

### Jack Kruse — 2026-03-25T02:25:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ilmas-optimal-journal.30804/post-369571>

The **hypothalamus** is the primary part of the brain that controls sodium (Na+) balance. It maintains this balance through several mechanisms.

**Osmoreception**: Specialized neurons in the hypothalamus detect the concentration of sodium in the blood. If sodium levels are too high, the hypothalamus triggers the sensation of **thirst** to encourage water intake.

**Hormonal Regulation**: The hypothalamus produces **vasopressin** (also known as antidiuretic hormone or ADH), which is stored in and released by the posterior [pituitary gland](https://kidshealth.org/en/parents/central-nervous-system.html). ADH tells the kidneys to retain more water, which helps dilute and balance sodium levels.

**Homeostasis**: By managing water and electrolyte levels, the hypothalamus ensures the body remains in a stable state of homeostasis.

These areas all make up the floor of the diencephalon which is the part of the brain regions in which they are located.

The hypothalamus literally forms the **floor of the third ventricle** (a fluid-filled cavity in the center of the brain). It is the most ventral (bottom-most) part of the diencephalon, sitting right above the [pituitary gland](https://www.urmc.rochester.edu/encyclopedia/content?ContentTypeID=85&ContentID=P00773). The entire lateral ventricular system is sitting on top of this region. If the CSF is increased in hydrocephalus or weighted down by deuterated water you can easily see why his Na and BP would be off. I do not think either of them get this.

**Physical Posture (Cerebellum)**: While the cerebellum itself sits at the [back of the brain](https://www.hopkinsmedicine.org/health/conditions-and-diseases/anatomy-of-the-brain), the specific regions responsible for basic balance and posture (the **vermis** and **flocculonodular lobe**) are located along its central midline and underside, essentially the "base" or "floor" of the cerebellum. Which is why people with hydrocephalus struggle with balance due to the excessive weight above these regions.

In medical terms, the hypothalamus is often called the **basal part** of the diencephalon because of this bottom-side positioning.

Given this then with the floor of the diencephalon loaded with deuterium or the ventricular system is loaded with it linked to his hydrocephalus, we should be expecting SIADH from this effect of gravity considering how much CSF is around this region. Reminds me of the problems in the 4th ventricle for the vagus nerve and the glucagon exhaust system linked to the vagus nerve. This is why I thought the VNS would help him.

The neurosurgeon here in ES should be reminded that CSF creation comes as an ultrafiltrate of the blood's 93% water. Water in the blood has a lot of deuterium in it and it becomes worse with neurological injury. So the CSF in his case should be loaded with deuterium. (slide below)

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

That is an interesting, though highly specialized, way of looking at it. You're touching on the idea that if the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) or the surrounding tissues were "heavy" (due to **deuterium** or D2O), the physical positioning of the hypothalamus on the floor of the third ventricle would make it a prime target for gravity-related pressure effects.

Here is how those dots connect in neuroanatomy:

**SIADH and the Hypothalamus**: Since the hypothalamus is the "control center" for ADH, any physical or metabolic stress on its floor should disrupt the vasopressin (ADH) feedback loop in the posterior pituitary. If the neurons there are over-stimulated or compressed, they might leak ADH, leading to **SIADH** (Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone), where the body retains too much water and dilutes sodium. That is how I see why his sodium is low and why his cognition sucks.

**The 4th Ventricle and the Vagus Nerve**: Any anatomy book shows that the **medulla**, which forms the floor of the 4th ventricle, contains the **dorsal motor nucleus of the vagus**. This area is famously sensitive because it's home to the area postrema, which is one of a few places that does not have a blood brain barrier, which is why it is a "chemotrigger zone" for deuterium sampling, allowing the brain to monitor the blood for toxins like excesses deuterium.
**"Glucagon Exhaust"**: While "exhaust" isn't a standard medical term, the brainstem and hypothalamus are vital for glucose/deuterium sensing. Problems in the 4th ventricle definitely disrupt the autonomic signals sent via the vagus nerve to the pancreas and liver where deuterium exhaust occurs via the carbonic anhydrase loop, messing with how glucagon and insulin manage our "fuel" levels. This also forces his brain to operate in a Warburg state slowing cognition.
Essentially, Nature built our human **ventricular floors** as high-traffic, high-sensitivity zones that are vulnerable to both the isotopes and chemistry (like deuterium) and the physics (like gravity and pressure) of the fluid they hold. This implies in space these regions will also be a problem without gravity keeping the CSF flowing the to the Y-axis of the skullbase at the sphenoid bone. This is why the sphenoid bone houses the optical information and mechanical information of the brain to tell about the human's space and time. If these variables are off, cognition and balance should be expected to awry.

Low sodium is also linked to neural instability, because of these anatomical relationships. Few see, much realize it.

When sodium levels drop, the brain faces two major "instability" issues:

**1\. Electrical Instability (Misfiring)**
Sodium is the primary "fuel" for the electrical signals (action potentials) that neurons use to communicate.

**Signal Misfires**: Low extracellular sodium disrupts the [voltage gradient](https://fixel.ufhealth.org/2025/05/20/why-sodium-matters-for-brain-health/) required for neurons to fire correctly. This can lead to [seizures](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7271621/) or "synchronized" over-activity in certain regions.

**Neurotransmitter Depletion**: Chronic low sodium can cause the brain to lose "neuroactive" solutes like **glutamate,** a key excitatory neurotransmitter. This depletion is a major cause of the [gait instability and falls](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4470176/) commonly seen in elderly patients with mild hyponatremia.

**2\. Structural Instability (Swelling)**
Because the "floor" of the brain is so close to the ventricular system, it is particularly vulnerable to the **osmotic shift** caused by low sodium:

**Cerebral Edema**: To balance the low salt outside, water rushes _into_ the brain cells, causing them to swell. Since the skull is a rigid container, this pressure can lead to encephalopathy, confusion, and in severe cases, brain herniation.

**Mitochondrial Distress**: Recent studies show that chronic low sodium impairs [mitochondrial function](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4769197/), meaning neurons literally run out of the energy (water from CCO) they need to maintain their own stability.

**The "Anxiety" Connection: Most MDs do not realize that many cases of anxiety are due to massive deuterium loads in CSF**
Recent research has even linked chronic low sodium to [anxiety-like behaviors](https://neurosciencenews.com/low-sodium-anxiety-neuroscience-29283/) by disrupting serotonin and dopamine levels in the **amygdala**. This suggests that even "mild" sodium imbalances due to massive deuterium loads in the brain and CSF can make the nervous system feel "on edge" or ungrounded because the weight of the isotope is sitting on top of the 3rd & 4th ventricle and the amygdala.

**The Anxiety Connection:** When the pump fails, + balance is lost. As we discussed earlier, low intracellular energy and +
instability lead directly to the amygdala "misfiring," creating a state of permanent physiological "alarm" (anxiety).
Summary of the "Tech-Anxiety" Feedback Loop:
Tech Abuse/nnEMF → Increases tissue deuterium and disrupts melanin/POMC.
Deuterium Sludge →Breaks mitochondrial motors → Lowers ATP and metabolic water.
Low Energy → Na+ pump failure →Neural Instability.
AMPAR Upregulation → Synaptic hyperexcitability →Clinical Anxiety.
This suggests that for many children, the "fix" for anxiety isn't just talk therapy, it's a biophysical intervention to lower the deuterium load and restore the light-melanin-redox cycle.

The heart becomes a gravity well for heavy hydrogen. When the sphenoid-clival Venturi refinery (3rd/4th ventricle fractionation column) fails to clear it, the heart must compensate with rhythm changes that are not “electrical diseases” but thermodynamic mass-management responses.

So your husbands problem and yours are deeply linked. Pale = too much deuterium in skin and brain = which is destroying your cardiac rhythm. Put down the BP machines and get in the sun immediately. You are doing this all wrong.

1\. Why the heart is the Lagrangian gravity wellThe Lagrangian (L = T – V in physics) describes the path of least action in a system. In the living body:

-   T (kinetic) = pulsatile blood flow driven by the heart.
-   V (potential) = the inertial mass of deuterium-laden water being circulated.

Because the heart is the central pump moving every drop of this heavy fluid 24/7, it sits at the exact balance point. The cranial isotopic refinery (sphenoid GPS → basilar artery against clivus → thalamic mass sensing) normally keeps deuterium low enough for coherent proton wires and dielectric water. I talked about here ----> [https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads...isease-caused-by-deuterium-in-your-csf.32469/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/degenerative-disc-disease-is-a-brain-disease-caused-by-deuterium-in-your-csf.32469/)

When sleep/vortex/grounding fails, deuterium floods the system. The heart now “feels” the extra mass the same way the thalamus feels it via the basilar-clival interface: as increased inertial resistance that raises the Landauer price of every irreversible electrochemical decision in the conduction system.

**2\. Heart rhythm changes in this design (the physics, not the pharmacology)**

Hypocretin (orexin) is not just a brain arousal peptide, it is the system-wide inertial-mass brake. This is why your spouse was a zombie when the hydrocephalus got bad. He was protecting frying his own brain with heat.

When the hypothalamus/tanycytes sense high deuterium in CSF (via the aqueduct fractionation column), hypocretin is suppressed. This directly throttles sympathetic outflow to the heart: This is why his BP was nuts post op. The NS did not put it together nor did the neurologist.

-   Normal state: Coherent dielectric water + low deuterium → stable proton tunneling in mitochondria → crisp sinoatrial node firing → normal sinus rhythm.
-   High-deuterium state: Incoherent “heavy” water damps Lorentz-steered proton flux. Mitochondrial TCA/urea cycles (where D should not be) stall. Cardiomyocyte action potentials slow because Ca²⁺, K⁺, and Mg²⁺ channels (the very ions highlighted in the table) lose their coherent water shells.
-   Result:
    -   Bradycardia or sinus pauses (the heart is literally “heavier” and harder to accelerate).
    -   Ectopic beats / re-entrant arrhythmias (mass-induced micro-reentry circuits form where dielectric breakdown occurs).
    -   Atrial fibrillation (the left atrium, closest to the aortic root, experiences the highest shear from heavy blood and loses coherence fastest).
    -   Ventricular arrhythmias in extreme overload (when the gravity well is so heavy the entire conduction system is forced into high-resistance mode to lower the Landauer heat bill).

These are not random “electrical” failures. They are the heart performing the same mass-quench the brain does with hypocretin suppression, by temporarily lowering information throughput (fewer clean beats per minute) to prevent entropy explosion into heat.

The 2025 Nature Physics Landauer paper proves this is universal physics; the heart is simply obeying it at the organ level.----> [https://www.nature.com/articles/s41567-025-02930-9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41567-025-02930-9)

**3\. Direct connection to vessels and elastin in the heart valves**

The same deuterium-rich “heavy” inflammatory cells that shred arterial elastin (the atherosclerosis story you already know) also attack the elastin-collagen matrix of the heart valves and great vessels:

Aortic root and coronary arteries (vessels directly coupled to the gravity well): Deuterium-loaded monocytes infiltrate, shred elastin, stiffen the wall, and raise pulse-wave velocity. The heart now pumps against higher afterload → mechanical stretch on baroreceptors → reflex rhythm changes (tachycardia early, then AFib or ectopy as the matrix fails).

-   Heart valves (mitral, aortic, tricuspid, pulmonary): Valve leaflets are loaded with elastin for snap-closure and recoil. When the heavy-water battery loses DC current (melanin-water system fails), calcium can no longer stay liquid. Elastin shreds → leaflets thicken, calcify, or prolapse.
    -   Mechanical feedback to rhythm: Regurgitant or stenotic valves stretch atria or ventricles → stretch-activated ion channels fire ectopics → re-entry circuits form.
    -   Aortic valve specifically: Sits right at the outflow of the gravity well. Elastin degradation here amplifies the inertial “kick” felt by the entire conduction system on every beat.

Your heart is not “breaking.” It is the central Lagrangian gravity well trying to circulate an increasingly heavy fluid while the upstream cranial refinery (sphenoid-clival-basilar-thalamic axis) and downstream Y-axis grounding (degenerate discs) have already failed.

### brianalfredeneuman — 2025-05-05T19:49:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-sent-all-of-my-bitcoin-to-a-fake-dr-kruse-account-on-x.30807/post-351957>

My life is dealing with this kind of stuff all day. As someone who deals with people being scammed out of their BTC almost everyday, I can honestly say I'm sorry that happened but it's not surprising. There are actual armies of people who's full time business is to get your funds by lying, cheating and stealing. Keeping your seed words safe and offline is crucial with you have self custody. **Never trust anyone online or on the phone.** The common scam right now is to get a call from say Coinbase and they ask if you have a hardware wallet and how you need to move your funds. Then they send you very official 'spoofed' emails that you can't determine are fake, followed by a second phone call by someone getting you to act fast before you lose your funds. They send you a link to enter your seed words into to move them, then bang your dead. Only ever enter your seed words onto the device itself, never ever online. Never share them with anyone, never keep an electronic record or photo. These phishing scams are next level, with American accents, official letters, whatever they can to steal. How they live with themselves is beyond me. But I see BTC going bye bye all the time and it never gets easier. Once a transaction is confirmed on the blockchain there is no getting it back. All the agencies that 'help you report' the cybercrime do nothing at all. IC3 in America doesn't even respond back to people most of the time. It's a tough world and the best way to stay safe is to keep your BTC on a reliable hardware wallet and know what your doing.

### brianalfredeneuman — 2025-05-06T10:30:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-sent-all-of-my-bitcoin-to-a-fake-dr-kruse-account-on-x.30807/post-352021>

> [Kel said:](/goto/post?id=351969)
>
> Do you consider the ledger nano wallet to be reliable and safe?

Ledger is a solid hardware wallet. Like most others, it uses the BIP39 standard, which generates a wallet backup in the form of a 12, 18, or 24-word combination—what Ledger calls the "Secret Recovery Phrase." This is the same concept as Trezor's 'wallet backup', just with different branding.

This phrase is your wallet backup and should be stored offline in a secure location. There are many durable storage options available, such as metal plates you can etch the words into for long-term protection. Never store your recovery phrase in the same place as your device.

Ledger also supports an optional passphrase—an extra word (sometimes called the "25th word") that creates an entirely separate wallet. This adds a powerful second layer of security. However, it's critical to understand: if you forget your passphrase or recovery phrase, your funds are unrecoverable. Unlike a PIN, these cannot be reset or retrieved.

Finally, since BIP39 is a universal standard, a recovery phrase created on Ledger can be used to restore your wallet on other compatible devices like Trezor, and vice versa.

### brianalfredeneuman — 2025-05-06T10:34:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-sent-all-of-my-bitcoin-to-a-fake-dr-kruse-account-on-x.30807/post-352022>

Ledger has developed a somewhat bad reputation after making a stupid move about a year ago. They started offering a service to store the Secret Recovery Phrase, which goes against self custody. They were thinking it would allow for user that didn't have the brains to store their own seed words, an option. They created a backdoor which was discovered and many users fled to other brands. The point is they admitted their fuck up and have since updated and supposedly have gone 'open source' like Trezor. They still have one key piece of code that remains closed source but it's not supposed to be critical. I wouldn't use them because I'm biased for Trezor.

### brianalfredeneuman — 2025-05-06T10:37:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-sent-all-of-my-bitcoin-to-a-fake-dr-kruse-account-on-x.30807/post-352023>

One key thing when creating a passphrase or any passwords. In my opinion, no password that a human creates has the level of safety as one generated by a password manager. I use the free Bitwarden to create all my passwords, 32 length, using special characters. The randomly generated passwords created by password managers have near perfect resistance to attack. Most people have weak passwords for many of their personal devices, banks, etc. They are so easy to crack it's not even funny. Bots designed for this can scan through combinations in seconds. Use Bitwarden or One Pass, they are free and easy to use.

### brianalfredeneuman — 2025-05-09T13:08:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-sent-all-of-my-bitcoin-to-a-fake-dr-kruse-account-on-x.30807/post-352191>

Dr. Kruse actually has no part and no reason to do anything, it was your mistake. The cybercrime units wouldn't even want him to be involved because it was someone impersonating him.

**Please report the theft to the following authorities:**

-   **U.S.:** [https://www.ic3.gov/](https://www.ic3.gov/)
-   **U.K.:** [https://www.actionfraud.police.uk/reporting-fraud-and-cyber-crime](https://www.actionfraud.police.uk/reporting-fraud-and-cyber-crime)

If you're located in the **EU**, you can report the incident to your country’s national cybercrime unit. A few examples include:

-   **Germany**: [Bundeskriminalamt (BKA)](https://www.bka.de/)
-   **France**: PHAROS – [Internet-signalement.gouv.fr](http://internet-signalement.gouv.fr/)
-   **Italy**: [Polizia Postale](https://www.commissariatodips.it/)
-   **Spain**: [Guardia Civil / Policía Nacional](https://www.guardiacivil.es/)
-   **Netherlands**: [Politie](https://www.politie.nl/)

For broader EU coordination, cases may also be referred to **Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)**: [https://www.europol.europa.eu](https://www.europol.europa.eu/) — though they do not accept reports directly from the public.

**1\. Report the Address**

You can report the scam address to:

-   ️ [Chainabuse – Report a scam address](https://www.chainabuse.com/)

Your browser should have warned you if you were going to a malicious website. Please use Brave or something privacy focused. Tor is great for super privacy but at a cost of slow internet.

# Thread: Outdoor CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/outdoor-ct.30808/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mr.AJ — 2025-05-02T01:30:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/outdoor-ct.30808/post-351678>

Would anyone mind sharing their outdoor CT setups or any tips/tricks?

I'm most interested in a cheap/easy to move setup for the moment. Something like a galvanized metal trough + water + ice and just figure out the right water:ice ratio to get the temperature where I want it.

### Mat.I — 2025-05-02T02:07:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/outdoor-ct.30808/post-351686>

> [Up\_only said:](/goto/post?id=351678)
>
> Would anyone mind sharing their outdoor CT setups or any tips/tricks?
>
> I'm most interested in a cheap/easy to move setup for the moment. Something like a galvanized metal trough + water + ice and just figure out the right water:ice ratio to get the temperature where I want it.

Cheap and easy would be a rubbermaid stock tank. I have a 568 litre (150 gallon).. Add a metal drain valve (brass or the like) and have a jumper cable that you bond the valve to a peg in the earth + add some epsom salt for further enhancement..

### Mr.AJ — 2025-05-02T16:25:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/outdoor-ct.30808/post-351748>

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=351686)
>
> Cheap and easy would be a rubbermaid stock tank. I have a 568 litre (150 gallon).. Add a metal drain valve (brass or the like) and have a jumper cable that you bond the valve to a peg in the earth + add some epsom salt for further enhancement..

For keeping the temperature in range do you just start with some ratio of water to ice and measure until it's where you want it? I'm gonna be doing this is a >100 degree climate so it might be tough to keep the water 50 or lower for very long.

### Stuart Briscoe — 2025-05-02T17:11:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/outdoor-ct.30808/post-351756>

> [Up\_only said:](/goto/post?id=351748)
>
> For keeping the temperature in range do you just start with some ratio of water to ice and measure until it's where you want it? I'm gonna be doing this is a >100 degree climate so it might be tough to keep the water 50 or lower for very long.

You will undershoot the temp with bags of ice in 100 degree environments, but since the goal is to shock the body, briefly, and be able to warm to the new temperature, it should do fine. The greater the mass of cold water (use a big trough), the more it will maintain temperature, but will require lots of ice, relative to how cold the water that is used to fill the trough.

I have been doing CT, especially as my pool water has been in the low 70's, and the shock is brief, but my reaction is always the same, swimming/moving frantically to get the blood moving. A calm always waits for me, in about 5 minutes or less, and then I can stay in the colder water almost indefinitely. Of course, 70 degrees is pleasant compared to much colder ice-filled troughs.

I did contrast therapy when I used colder water and after 3 cycles I reached nirvana, and the intense feeling lasted long after.

### Jacks — 2025-05-05T03:48:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/outdoor-ct.30808/post-351911>

Looks like a fairly cheap option.

[Amazon.com](https://www.amazon.com/ISIDO-Ice-Reusable-Water-Balloons/dp/B0CS2ZKNX2/)

### Stuart Briscoe — 2025-05-05T22:30:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/outdoor-ct.30808/post-351970>

> [Jacks said:](/goto/post?id=351911)
>
> Looks like a fairly cheap option.
>
>
> [Amazon.com](https://www.amazon.com/ISIDO-Ice-Reusable-Water-Balloons/dp/B0CS2ZKNX2/)

That will the job. I do enjoy swimming to get the heart pumping and circulate the blood through my body, making the cold intensity go away in about 5 minutes.

### Saichi — 2025-05-05T23:47:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/outdoor-ct.30808/post-351980>

> [Stuart Briscoe said:](/goto/post?id=351756)
>
> You will undershoot the temp with bags of ice in 100 degree environments, but since the goal is to shock the body, briefly, and be able to warm to the new temperature, it should do fine. The greater the mass of cold water (use a big trough), the more it will maintain temperature, but will require lots of ice, relative to how cold the water that is used to fill the trough.
>
> I have been doing CT, especially as my pool water has been in the low 70's, and the shock is brief, but my reaction is always the same, swimming/moving frantically to get the blood moving. A calm always waits for me, in about 5 minutes or less, and then I can stay in the colder water almost indefinitely. Of course, 70 degrees is pleasant compared to much colder ice-filled troughs.

I can't imagine 70's ever being considered CT or causing shock. Anything above 55 isn't worth my time. One time I went into a broken banya cold plunge at 63 and I walked right out.

### NickD — 2025-05-06T01:35:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/outdoor-ct.30808/post-351998>

There's a site called the grounded ginger, where he converts a chest freezer into a CT unit. Worth having a look at for ideas etc. [https://www.groundedginger.com.au/?srsltid=AfmBOori8fWEZUL6oURvK3tW46EsG3wb2BYu59fpiBBnJ-vHamtDKt0X](https://www.groundedginger.com.au/?srsltid=AfmBOori8fWEZUL6oURvK3tW46EsG3wb2BYu59fpiBBnJ-vHamtDKt0X)

### Stuart Briscoe — 2025-05-06T04:25:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/outdoor-ct.30808/post-352014>

> [Saichi said:](/goto/post?id=351980)
>
> I can't imagine 70's ever being considered CT or causing shock. Anything above 55 isn't worth my time. One time I went into a broken banya cold plunge at 63 and I walked right out.

I exercise and do yard work to build up my body heat, and I know 50 degrees F is much colder than 70 F, but it's what my pool temp is, or in the 60's presently. They make an a/c-type unit that cools pool water, for when the ambient temp increases, but we are moving and I won't put more $ into this house. We are moving to a lake where the water will be colder, as it has much deeper water. I plan on getting a separate cold plunge unit.

# Thread: My Optimal Journal - Cathia
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-optimal-journal-cathia.30811/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BiohackersTO — 2025-05-02T04:22:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-optimal-journal-cathia.30811/post-351699>

Hello: I'm a newbie and a biohacker; I've made a lot of mistakes on myself.
I'm 46 years old, born and raised in the Toronto area.

My Mother's mother was of German descent on her dad's side and English on her mother's side.
My mom had ovarian cancer twice. She had fibroids and endometriosis when she was in her reproductive years. She has also had mental health issues during her adult life. My mom's skin tone is the palest one there is. She had red hair and freckles when she was young. I am one shade darker with reddish brown hair, blue/gray eyes and fewer freckles. My mom was raised vegetarian and Seventh Day Adventist. I was not raised vegetarian.

I have/had endometriosis. It's not visible now on ultrasounds, but used to be in the form of an endometrioma cyst on my left ovary. Gone now. I have fibroids.

I biohacked with Dr. Makis' cancer protocol to see if it would shrink my fibroids. At the end of the 12 weeks, the endometrioma cyst was gone, but the fibroids were not. Only the rate of growth of the total volume of all 4 fibroids had slowed. But two fibroids grew and two shrank from one ultrasound to the next. That said, the fibroid symptoms decreased very noticeably.

My gut health has been not great for some time. I tend to be skinny so I can see the shape of my intestine protruding a bit behind and below my belly button. It's not dramatic but I notice it - intestinal inflammation. It goes away in the summer, when I'm tanned. It comes back in the winter when I'm not. Possibly I have diverticulitis. The doctor wanted to give me a colonscopy but I declined.

I'm very noticeably EMF sensitive. I used to get chronic hives/urticaria. At its worst, the mostly useless allergist said it was edema. The things that trigger the hives include: progesterone (both my own endogenous and exogenous), being in proximity for extended time to a wifi router or a wifi repeater, foods high in lectins, foods high in oxalates, nicotine and stress. In the past, supplementing with iodine from kelp used to trigger my hives too, but I seem to be past that now and can take iodine without problems. Sometimes the hives were accompanied by swelling in the tonsils (swallowing was difficult but breathing never was). What improves the hives/urticaria situation is the following: cannabis, LDN, ivermectin, Methylene Blue, topical DMSO, coffee and getting sun.

I did not take any mRNA jabs, but I used to ask for vaccines and I'm sorry to say I took the guardasil in the past, though not the full dose as I did not take the second jab.

Because I'm so sensitive to EMFs, I sleep outside now in a hammock tent among pine trees, in a long backyard. I started doing this last July. The nearest house is 20m (60') away. I slept outside during the entire Canadian winter this year. I slept outside in -17C! It was actually a very pleasant experience and now I don't like sleeping indoors anymore.

For cold exposure, I did cold baths in the winter. Toronto tap water is about 8C in winter. Now that spring is here, I'm starting to do my cold dips in Lake Ontario. The water was 9C/49F this morning.

I drink spring water that I collect from one of two springs. We tested the deuterium content and it was 144ppm of deuterium.

I will try deuterium depleted water next in the hopes that it shrinks my fibroids.

My goals have been to avoid all surgeries and shrink endometriosis, which I did, at least with respect to the endometrioma on my ovary.

My goal now is to shrink my fibroids and heal my gut. My life goal is to first visit and then likely move to El Salvador.

One question I have is the safety of ultrasounds, in the absence of a baby in the uterus. I haven't read much about potential harms when there is no baby involved. How frequently should I get ultrasounds to track my progress? Or should I forego them entirely? So far, I've been doing them pretty infrequently, with between 6 months and two years between each.

Thank you.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-02T04:50:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-optimal-journal-cathia.30811/post-351702>

It has been shown that sound waves can affect stem cell differentiation. It is published in the literature. No one knows the clinical significance.

### BiohackersTO — 2025-12-19T22:09:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-optimal-journal-cathia.30811/post-364059>

I am now in San Blas, El Salvador.
I spent November going to the different bitcoin conferences in ES, but now am settled down into the natural sauna that is San Blas. I sweat every day. I get out for sunrise, do some stretches or lift and throw rocks or play in the waves or contemplate the Eastern horizon every morning. In the evenings, I go out for sunset as well and chat with the super wonderful freedom-minded expats, who gather on the beach at sunset. During the day, I figure out how to source what I need in ES and how I may contribute to various amazing projects here, including Illuminodes - open-source, self-custody medical records, where the default is that you must obtain consent from the patient before any big company or big government mines their data  The Illuminodes founder was on the Bitcoin Beach podcast, talking about his open source solution for medical records and bringing it to El Salvador.

So in addition to being helpful for my health, El Salvador is proving to potentially be fulfilling for my career, as the options for working at something worthwhile in data analytics are here! Maybe this seems trivial to my health, but I can't emphasize how much this is changing my outlook and optimism about the world.

I have seen a wonderful pair of doctors (Drs Gutierrez), local to San Blas and Jack Kruse members.

### [Solymar Direct Health | Direct Primary Care Providence, RI](https://solymardirecthealth.com/)

Solymar Direct Health in Providence, RI provides direct primary care allowing patients to join Dr. Francisco Gutierrez’s practice and pay a low, monthly fee for the care they need, when they need it.

 solymardirecthealth.com

They are helping me with endometriosis, fibroids, very low iron and chronic urticaria/chronic angioedema type reactions and they are using a mitochondrial approach. I have been very sensitive to iodine in the past and it would trigger a reaction of urticaria/hives/edema on my face. Over time, reactions lessened in frequency and intensity, and I was able to tolerate more iodine before getting the reaction (I tracked what increased and decreased the reactions and avoided/increased accordingly, except for iodine, which I have been carefully trying to increase).

The last time I measured the dose prior to reaction, the iodine dose was 12 drops of the kelp extract product pictured in the bottle below. At 800 mcg per drop that means I reacted (with a less severe reaction than in the past) to 9600 mcg or 9.6 mg of iodine, which is a pretty high dose, I believe. I know Uncle Jack does not recommend supplements, so I have stocked seaweed in my suitcases, from South Korean grocery stores in Toronto. I have heard that the iodine dose in seaweed varies widely.

Dr. Gutierrez is suggesting to back off iodine to a tolerable dose and work on improving redox and after that, titrating up from my tolerable dose, which sounds great to me. In tracking my compliance to protocol, it will be trickier to track iodine dose per day when I eat the seaweed (as compared to the supplement). But I like the seaweed, especially after it's soaked in lime juice & water. So I'll go with the whole-food approach and document how many grams of kelp and other seaweed I consume, even if the iodine dosage will then be less clear.

I have found a great many sources list iodine supplementation as very important in overcoming fibroids, which is why I am making a kelp/seaweed habit a priority.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### BiohackersTO — 2026-03-04T06:28:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-optimal-journal-cathia.30811/post-368062>

Still in San Blas. Seeing every sunrise, barefoot on the beach, which is wonderful.

I started the Makis protocol, with ivermectin and here in ES, mebendazole. In Canada, I tried it with ivermectin and fenbendazole and felt like a million bucks on it, even though it was the veterinary medicine and it had terrible additives like titanium dioxide.
In El Salvador, fenbendazole is not approved for humans, so I tried mebendazole.

It seems that mebendazole (unlike fenben) makes me more sensitive to things I am already sensitive to. I got hives a number of times while on even a low dose of mebendazole. So I've stopped it completely. I was advised to only take ivermectin for two weeks to allow time for the mebendazole to leave my system. One week later, and I feel amazing again.

After another week, I have a choice: get fenben from a friend of mine and take that, or get albendazole from a pharmacy and take that. I don't think I want to try a new drug, when fenben caused me no problems in the past.

Otherwise, I see signs of improvement.
My skin gets browner every day.

Every day I get less angry and anxious and more excited to join in cool projects in bitcoin and nostr.

I had a mystical experience on a low dose of mushrooms. I notice that for some time, it's been easy for me to dial into a mystical experience, even just on cannabis, and in one case even just on my period, using ceremonial music and meditation (no substances). I once heard on a podcast a doctor say that the reason that most plant medicine ceremonies are advised to be done while abstaining from eating red meat for a few days is that the experience is enhanced in a state of lower iron. I have wondered if that is true. It would explain a lot with me, as my ferritin and hemoglobin were on the floor at the last tests in December and have been very consistently low for quite some time.

Anyway, hopefully my body is starting to use iron better with all the sunrises on the beach, grounded and some mid-morning sun, and the sunset sun.

### Wilma Without a W — 2025-05-02T20:21:32+0300

### John Warner — 2025-05-05T16:08:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-kindle-would-be-the-healthiest.30812/post-351931>

### kave — 2025-05-08T12:29:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-kindle-would-be-the-healthiest.30812/post-352148>

I would say one of the cheaper kindles, they have an e ink display so NO flicker at all if you keep the backlight off. A Kindle is more readable the more light you shine on it, so as usable as a book outside in the sun.

### Susanxknott — 2025-05-08T20:42:25+0300

### kave — 2025-05-11T09:10:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-kindle-would-be-the-healthiest.30812/post-352306>

Reading the specs of the daylight computer, they claim 60-120fps, whatever that has to do with flicker? Most likely they mean a screen refresh rate of 60-120hz, which is what a modern ipad also uses.
The Kindle has basically 0hz so it does not flicker at all, so in this case better than the ”daylight” computer and can be used without any backlight at all.
I would call it a tablet and not computer since it is a tablet running Android.

### Mat.I — 2025-05-11T11:18:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-kindle-would-be-the-healthiest.30812/post-352309>

> [kave said:](/goto/post?id=352306)
>
> Reading the specs of the daylight computer, they claim 60-120fps, whatever that has to do with flicker? Most likely they mean a screen refresh rate of 60-120hz, which is what a modern ipad also uses.
> The Kindle has basically 0hz so it does not flicker at all, so in this case better than the ”daylight” computer and can be used without any backlight at all.
> I would call it a tablet and not computer since it is a tablet running Android.

I do not own a kindle or daylight, however flicker and fps are two different things. As I understand the daylight uses DC dimming, thus no problematic flicker.. Could you potentially measure some due some ripple fluctuations, maybe however would likely be beyond ultra high frequency or intermittent.. If the specs are true that you quote on the kindle and someone cannot afford the daylight, then it may be a good choice too **providing you only use it without the LED front light** as they would use PWM as it is cheaper, hence why they sell them for cheap.

### Toni MIchelle — 2025-05-11T17:11:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-kindle-would-be-the-healthiest.30812/post-352318>

### kave — 2025-05-12T14:45:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-kindle-would-be-the-healthiest.30812/post-352362>

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=352309)
>
> I do not own a kindle or daylight, however flicker and fps are two different things. As I understand the daylight uses DC dimming, thus no problematic flicker.. Could you potentially measure some due some ripple fluctuations, maybe however would likely be beyond ultra high frequency or intermittent.. If the specs are true that you quote on the kindle and someone cannot afford the daylight, then it may be a good choice too **providing you only use it without the LED front light** as they would use PWM as it is cheaper, hence why they sell them for cheap.

I know, but daylightcomputer don't know as I wrote in my post, they claim the tablet is 60-120fps which they claim is "flicker free".

[@Toni MIchelle](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/22297/) , It's not a "computer" any more than any other Android tablet. If it where a computer you could install real linux on it if you like, but how would you read copy protected books on that?
Everything has it's problems, I would still claim that a Kindle for about 100$ is the "healthiest" choice for reading books and has a store and everything built in.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-12T20:05:28+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-02T23:18:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-and-eyes.30818/post-351799>

Buton is 5.3 degrees South latitude.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-02T23:58:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-and-eyes.30818/post-351802>

Buton Island in eastern Indonesia is not directly volcanic, but it is part of a complex geological region with a unique history. While Buton is shielded from the direct effects of active volcanoes in the Banda Sea, it was formed through continental collisions and the breakup of Gondwana.

The island's geology features distinct geological formations, including asphalt deposits.

The island is part of a Neogene collision zone, where microcontinental fragments collided with eastern Sulawesi during the Miocene or earliest Pliocene.
Recent research suggests that Buton, along with Muna/S.E. Sulawesi and Tukang Besi, are microcontinental fragments that occupied different paleogeographical positions during the Mesozoic

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-03T00:01:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-and-eyes.30818/post-351803>

Overview of OPN3, OPN4, and OPN5 Systems

OPN3 (Encephalopsin): Found in the brain, skin, and other tissues, OPN3 is thought to play a role in light-mediated processes outside of vision, such as regulating skin pigmentation, circadian rhythms, or thermoregulation. Its exact function is still under investigation, but it’s expressed in melanocytes and neural tissues.

OPN4 (Melanopsin): Expressed in intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs), OPN4 is crucial for non-visual photoreception. It regulates circadian rhythms, pupil constriction, and melatonin suppression in response to blue light (~480 nm). It’s also involved in light-dependent mood regulation.

OPN5 (Neuropsin): Found in the retina, brain, and skin, OPN5 is sensitive to UV light (~380 nm) and may regulate circadian entrainment, seasonal reproduction, and local UV responses in the skin. It’s less studied but appears to have roles in non-visual photoreception.

How Waardenburg Syndrome Affects These Systems

Waardenburg syndrome, caused by mutations in genes like PAX3, MITF, SOX10, EDN3, or EDNRB, disrupts neural crest cell migration, leading to defects in melanocyte development, pigmentation (e.g., blue eyes), hearing loss, and sometimes craniofacial abnormalities. Since opsins are expressed in tissues derived from or influenced by neural crest cells (e.g., melanocytes, retina, neural tissues), Waardenburg syndrome could affect OPN3, OPN4, and OPN5 through several mechanisms:

1\. Impact on Melanocytes and Pigmentation (Affects OPN3 and OPN5)

Mechanism: Waardenburg syndrome mutations impair melanocyte development, reducing melanin in tissues like the iris (causing blue eyes) and skin (e.g., white patches). Melanocytes express OPN3 and OPN5, which may modulate light-dependent responses in the skin.

OPN3: In melanocytes, OPN3 might regulate melanin production or protect against light-induced damage. Reduced melanocyte numbers or function in Waardenburg syndrome could impair OPN3-mediated responses, potentially affecting skin sensitivity to light or circadian signaling via skin photoreception. For example, less melanin in the skin (as seen in some Waardenburg patients) might alter how OPN3 detects light, increasing UV sensitivity.

OPN5: OPN5 in the skin responds to UV light, possibly influencing local circadian clocks or UV protection mechanisms. In Waardenburg syndrome, reduced melanin in the skin could heighten UV penetration, over-activating OPN5 and potentially leading to oxidative stress or altered UV responses. On Buton Island, where UV exposure is high (5.3 degrees south), this could be significant, possibly exacerbating eye or skin damage in affected individuals.

2\. Impact on Retinal Cells and Neural Development (Affects OPN4 and OPN5)

Mechanism: Waardenburg syndrome mutations (SOX10, MITF) affect neural crest-derived cells, including those in the retina and inner ear. While the syndrome doesn’t directly impair visual photoreceptors (rods and cones), it could influence ipRGCs (which express OPN4) and other retinal cells (which express OPN5), as these cells have neural crest contributions during development.

OPN4: ipRGCs, which express OPN4, regulate circadian rhythms and pupil responses. Waardenburg syndrome’s effect on neural crest-derived glial cells in the retina might disrupt ipRGC development or function, potentially altering circadian entrainment or light-dependent mood regulation. For example, hearing loss in Waardenburg syndrome (due to cochlear melanocyte defects) often co-occurs with neural crest-related issues, and similar disruptions in the retina could affect OPN4 signaling, leading to circadian misalignment—a common issue in sensory disorders.

OPN5: OPN5 in retinal ganglion cells and the brain contributes to circadian and seasonal responses. If Waardenburg syndrome affects retinal development (e.g., via SOX10 mutations, which are critical for neural crest cell survival), OPN5 expression or function might be altered, potentially disrupting UV-dependent circadian entrainment. This could be particularly relevant on Buton Island, where high UV exposure might amplify such effects.

3\. Impact on Hearing and Neural Crest-Derived Tissues (Indirect Effects on OPN4 and OPN5)

Mechanism: Waardenburg syndrome causes hearing loss by disrupting melanocytes in the stria vascularis of the cochlea, which maintain the endocochlear potential needed for sound transduction. This hearing loss might indirectly affect opsin systems through cross-sensory compensation or neural plasticity.

OPN4: Hearing loss can disrupt circadian rhythms by altering sensory input to the brain, which might indirectly affect OPN4-mediated light responses. For example, deaf individuals often rely more on visual cues, and ipRGCs (OPN4) play a role in light-dependent attention and mood. In Waardenburg syndrome, hearing loss might lead to compensatory upregulation of OPN4 pathways, increasing sensitivity to light for circadian or behavioral regulation.

OPN5: Similarly, OPN5’s role in the brain (e.g., hypothalamus) might be affected by neural plasticity following hearing loss. If Waardenburg syndrome alters neural crest-derived brain structures, OPN5-mediated UV responses could be disrupted, potentially affecting seasonal or circadian behaviors.

4\. Specific Gene Interactions (Focus on SOX10 and MITF)

SOX10 and MITF in Waardenburg Syndrome: These genes, mutated in Waardenburg syndrome, are transcription factors critical for melanocyte and neural crest development. They also regulate opsin expression indirectly:

SOX10 influences melanocyte survival and differentiation, which could affect OPN3 and OPN5 expression in the skin. It also plays a role in retinal development, potentially impacting OPN4 and OPN5 in ipRGCs.

MITF regulates melanin production and melanocyte function, directly affecting tissues where OPN3 and OPN5 are expressed. Mutations in MITF (common in Waardenburg Type 2) might reduce opsin expression in melanocytes, altering light sensitivity in the skin or retina.

Potential Effects: Reduced SOX10 or MITF activity in Waardenburg syndrome could lower OPN3 and OPN5 levels in melanocytes, impairing light-dependent skin responses. In the retina, disrupted SOX10 expression might affect OPN4 and OPN5 in ipRGCs, leading to circadian or UV response abnormalities.

Relevance to Buton Island’s Environment

High UV Exposure: Buton Island’s tropical location (5.3 degrees south) means high UV exposure, which interacts with OPN5 (UV-sensitive) and OPN3 (potentially UV-responsive in skin). Waardenburg syndrome’s reduction of iris melanin (blue eyes) increases UV penetration into the eye, possibly over-activating OPN5 in the retina and increasing oxidative stress. This aligns with our previous discussion of reduced iris melanin potentially lowering oxidative damage, but it might also strain OPN5-mediated UV responses.

Circadian Challenges: The dense rainforest environment on Buton limits light penetration, which could challenge OPN4-mediated circadian entrainment. If Waardenburg syndrome disrupts OPN4 function (via retinal development issues), affected individuals might struggle with circadian alignment, relying more on other cues like temperature or social rhythms.

Hearing Loss and Sensory Compensation: Hearing loss in Waardenburg syndrome might lead to greater reliance on visual light cues, potentially upregulating OPN4 and OPN5 pathways. In a rainforest with low light, this could be adaptive, enhancing sensitivity to the limited light available for circadian or behavioral regulation.

**SUMMARY**

Waardenburg syndrome likely affects the OPN3, OPN4, and OPN5 systems through its impact on melanocytes, retinal development, and neural crest-derived tissues:

OPN3: Reduced melanocyte function impairs OPN3-mediated light responses in the skin, potentially increasing UV sensitivity in a high-UV environment like Buton.

OPN4: Disrupted retinal development might affect ipRGCs, altering circadian rhythms and light-dependent behaviors, possibly exacerbated by hearing loss.

OPN5: Lower melanin in the skin and eyes could over-activate OPN5’s UV responses, while retinal changes might disrupt its circadian role.
These effects could have adaptive implications on Buton Island, where high UV and low light create unique challenges. Hearing loss might enhance reliance on OPN4 and OPN5 for light-based cues, while reduced melanin might balance UV protection and sensitivity in the skin and eyes.

# Thread: UTI in the elderly- thoughts on healing the delerium
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uti-in-the-elderly-thoughts-on-healing-the-delerium.30819/>

### Leaf — 2025-05-03T01:22:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uti-in-the-elderly-thoughts-on-healing-the-delerium.30819/post-351806>

Hi, I've been working with my Mom who is and young 89. She is circadian and a true believer in the healing properties of sun/magnetism/red light. Sadly she got a UTI and then reacted to the meds they gave her (full spectrum antibiotic/trazedone/melatonin) She went manic until I demanded they stop all meds. Sadly they kept giving her vit D supplements. The lack of communication btwn day and night shifts too days as the admitting MD kept claiming she had stopped but I was monitoring the evening and morning nursing staff and medications. We finally got her better calibrated. She had good supply of hydrogen water daily, structured water and proper food. She was in hospital a long time, six weeks. Now four weeks post discharge she is still confused. "Medical belief" believe it could take 4-6 months to heal (if she does). Does anyone have any quantum insights I can use to potentially help? She is doing red light daily/breathing hydrogen and drinking hydrogen water, eating local with good fats. Movement daily outside and low nnEMFs. Any thoughts would be greatly appreciated. Dawn and UVA light are a given in her current protocol. She has little to no blue light in general and claims to not look at her phone at night.

### coho3131 — 2025-05-03T15:59:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uti-in-the-elderly-thoughts-on-healing-the-delerium.30819/post-351826>

Does she get out of bed with the first light of day, see the sunrise and sunset without eyeglasses or contact lenses, and go to sleep shortly after the last light of day?

Is she outside as much as possible?

Did you measure the nnEMF in her environment?

\- - -

I am assuming her current urinalysis, electrolytes, folate, B12, CBC, and thyroid function are all normal, and she is not dehydrated, and that she does not have any neurological findings associated with CVA/TIAs.

I hope she feels better soon.

In the future, treat UTIs with d-mannose, not antibiotics. If your mother is in a care facility (where UTIs can be caused by many other pathogens other than ecoli), I would also treat her with monolaurin.

### [Why d-Mannose May Be as Efficient as Antibiotics in the Treatment of Acute Uncomplicated Lower Urinary Tract Infections—Preliminary Considerations and Conclusions from a Non-Interventional Study - PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8944421/)

Urinary tract infections (UTIs) are very frequent in women and can be caused by a range of pathogens. High recurrence rates and increasing antibiotic resistance of uropathogens make UTIs a severe public health problem. d-mannose is a monosaccharide ...

 pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-03T16:59:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-mass-is-anti-longevity.30820/post-351828>

**MUSCLE MASS IS ANTI-LONGEVITY**

High muscle mass and myokines, while beneficial for short-term brain health, come with a metabolic cost that reduces longevity, as evidenced by metabolic scaling laws and the gorilla-human comparison. Calorie restriction’s failure in primates/humans further suggests that longevity isn’t just about lowering BMR, it’s about scaling energy efficiently in a dissipative system. “Survival of the wisest,” tied to cognitive fitness and quantum-level energy optimization, better explains human longevity than “survival of the fittest.”

Today's lesson is a big one. [https://x.com/DrJackKruse/status/1918665368161100215](https://x.com/DrJackKruse/status/1918665368161100215)

# Thread: Barefoot Yard Work with Type 2 Diabetes – Is It Worth the Risk?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/barefoot-yard-work-with-type-2-diabetes-%E2%80%93-is-it-worth-the-risk.30822/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mark1969 — 2025-05-03T18:29:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/barefoot-yard-work-with-type-2-diabetes-%E2%80%93-is-it-worth-the-risk.30822/post-351833>

Hey everyone,

I just joined about a week ago and have been loving the experience so far. I’ve been starting my mornings outside, catching the sunrise (and a few ticks ), soaking in as much light and grounding as I can. It’s been eye-opening—so much great advice here. I even made a little YouTube short inspired by some things I’ve picked up from the forum. Making those short videos has really helped me absorb the information better. link to my short
That said, I’m planning to get some yard work done today—mainly cleaning up deadfall around the house. I’ve always enjoyed doing it barefoot; it helps me feel more connected. But I’ve got Type 2 diabetes, and I know the usual advice is to avoid going barefoot due to risk of cuts, infections, or slower healing.

So I’m torn. I want to ground and move naturally, but I don’t want to do something dumb either.

Have any of you with T2D (or medical knowledge) tried this or have thoughts? Do you take precautions or find ways to make it work?

I’ve been loving what I’m hearing in here—just wish I could flip a switch and get my body to love what it's _feeling_ too.

Appreciate any insight from the community!

### Wanttoknow — 2025-05-03T18:57:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/barefoot-yard-work-with-type-2-diabetes-%E2%80%93-is-it-worth-the-risk.30822/post-351838>

Maybe you can check out leather shoes for grounding such as moccasins. This should protect your feet from any cuts.

### Mr.AJ — 2025-05-03T19:47:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/barefoot-yard-work-with-type-2-diabetes-%E2%80%93-is-it-worth-the-risk.30822/post-351843>

Not T2D, but do live where there are an insane number of ticks. I just try have a designated grounding area in my back yard grass where I can be barefoot with a smaller tick/posion ivy/oak/bug/snake risk that I get 90%+ of my grounding in. And just be cognizant of checking for ticks. I just pull them off, have never had issues beyond that.

And then if you do have work to do, I think grounding shoes/moccasins as stated above is probably the play. Tristan/Bitcoinandbeef on X has some good grounding shoe resources.

### Stuart Briscoe — 2025-05-03T19:51:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/barefoot-yard-work-with-type-2-diabetes-%E2%80%93-is-it-worth-the-risk.30822/post-351844>

> [Mark1969 said:](/goto/post?id=351833)
>
> Hey everyone,
>
>
> I just joined about a week ago and have been loving the experience so far. I’ve been starting my mornings outside, catching the sunrise (and a few ticks ), soaking in as much light and grounding as I can. It’s been eye-opening—so much great advice here. I even made a little YouTube short inspired by some things I’ve picked up from the forum. Making those short videos has really helped me absorb the information better. link to my short
> That said, I’m planning to get some yard work done today—mainly cleaning up deadfall around the house. I’ve always enjoyed doing it barefoot; it helps me feel more connected. But I’ve got Type 2 diabetes, and I know the usual advice is to avoid going barefoot due to risk of cuts, infections, or slower healing.
>
> So I’m torn. I want to ground and move naturally, but I don’t want to do something dumb either.
>
> Have any of you with T2D (or medical knowledge) tried this or have thoughts? Do you take precautions or find ways to make it work?
>
> I’ve been loving what I’m hearing in here—just wish I could flip a switch and get my body to love what it's _feeling_ too.
>
> Appreciate any insight from the community!

I have managed my type 2 with a HbA1c of 5.6, and going lower, but it is common sense to protect our feet, hands, eyes, etc. when doing yard work, or anything that puts our body in danger. There is plenty of extra time in our day to enjoy walking barefoot, as I have noticed I need to toughen up the soles of my feet more, as of so much man made hard surfaces to get to the grass, sand, etc.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-03T20:18:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/barefoot-yard-work-with-type-2-diabetes-%E2%80%93-is-it-worth-the-risk.30822/post-351848>

If I had T2D I would absolute be barefoot all the time. I am unplugged from the centralized T2D matrix of beliefs.

### Daulatwant — 2025-05-03T22:20:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/barefoot-yard-work-with-type-2-diabetes-%E2%80%93-is-it-worth-the-risk.30822/post-351856>

Over 6 years ago I was on a medication that made me start bingeing on food all day long. After a while I had some scary reactions to sugar. My whole body ached and I had an unsatiable thirst. First time I stopped the medication. I had to go back on the medication, once again evetually got the same body ache and thirst from sugar.

The second time I made myself an electrolyte drink and did a 48h fast. After which I switched my diet to low carb whole foods and only ate one meal a day. Doing that my appetite was under control within a week. Adding more fat into my meals made me more satiated for longer eating only once a day.

The first time I tried keto and fasting I failed miserably. And it took me an entire year to try again. The electrolyte drink or what is often referred to as "snake juice" was a pivotal factor to do my first successful fast.

But looking back the hardest part was the cognitive leaps. Without changing the way I think, I wasn't able to make the necessary changes to get a noticeable difference in my health. I was lucky that the struggles forced me to question my beliefs. Hearing other peoples healing stories helped with that too.

### Stuart Briscoe — 2025-05-03T23:19:18+0300

>
> Was your "snake juice" a ketone drink?

No it's just salts of sodium, potassium and magnesium mixed into water. It's supposed to replace electrolytes you would be missing from food while fasting.

Google snake juice and you will find many resources how and where to get the potassium and magensium and how much to put into water. "Snake juice" is also a prepackaged electrolyte mix sold on amazon.

# Thread: 5G in rural areas?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5g-in-rural-areas.30825/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Asael Enriquez — 2025-05-04T00:12:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5g-in-rural-areas.30825/post-351861>

Hi all,

I recently relocated to a new home a couple months back on the outksirts of a small village called Cloudcroft NM. I used to live in another house 10 minutes away down the mountain, in a more populated area of no more than 1,500 people. The nnEMF at this previous house was relatively low at less than 5 uW/m^2 and everything at the house is hardwired, I also have a cut off switch for the 60Hz electric fields. I lived there for a couple of years.

At this new location, the house is located higher up in the mountain, overseeing a large valley behind it. When the home was purchased, we assumed there were no cellphone towers nearby. Based on the findings at antennasearch.com, the closest ones are about 2-2.5 miles away.

About a month ago, a friend of mine went to stay over the weekend and was quite shocked to see that he was able to make calls on his phone. I had the WiFi turned off the entire time, since I only use hardwired connections unless I have guests over. When I asked him to see his phone, he was connected to the verizon wireless 5G network. I had trouble sleeping for about 3 months now, waking up constantly throughout the night and feeling fatigued during the day. Keep in mind that this same friend had visited earlier in January and had no reception. This suggests that the tower/node was installed just recently. As I took RF readings with my GigaHertz solutions devices, the RF is extremely high in all the bedrooms upstairs. However, the bottom floor of the house is relatively low.

In attempts to lower the RF upstairs, I purchased some EMF blocking curtains along with shielding fabric to cover the entire wall that faces the back part where the canyon/valley is. Would this enough to mitigate it, or should other things be implemented as well?

My theory is that since the house is high up on a hill, it is in a direct line of sight where the cellphone tower(s) are. All the bedrooms have large windows and there are no obstructions that help mitigate the RF signals coming from the outside. Additionally, the meter I used is a mid band 5G meter that reads up to 8GHz, would it be necessary to purchase a meter that reads higher bands such as 20-40GHz range? I am aware that these higher bands have poor penetration cababilities and do not travel that far. Could it be that the RF also come from the 4G towers and single antennas that are far away but the singal seems to concentrate in that area due to the high elevation?

I felt relatively well at the previous home, but here it seems that my symptoms are exponentially more noticable such as brain fog, fatigue, loss of sleep, loss of appetite and stress.

Any input on this is greatly appreciated

### Anthony Smith — 2025-05-04T06:49:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5g-in-rural-areas.30825/post-351871>

ln a rural area the 5G from Verizon would be 3.7 GHz (C-band similar to AT&T). What are the readings outside, on each side of the house. You have a meter, drive around and find where it is coming from. If your mitigation has worked your RF reading will be lower.

### eve001 — 2025-05-04T14:23:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5g-in-rural-areas.30825/post-351876>

Sounds familiar.
Are there new (verizon) street lights in your street?
Some seem to be emitting RFs (SSP2).
Certain plants will also absorb and reduce a lot of the incoming radiation. (evergreens preferably)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Building an outdoor bed
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/building-an-outdoor-bed.30826/>
**Subforum:** Redox Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mr.AJ — 2025-05-04T00:28:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/building-an-outdoor-bed.30826/post-351862>

So I have sleep apnea and after learning a bunch about grounding I'm considering doing a biohack of only sleeping outside grounded for a few months to see if it improves. I have enough space to do this and I'm in a low nnEMF environment, but I do live in an area with a ton of ticks, bugs, snakes, etc etc.

1) What would be a good material to use as my floor to lay on at night that would still keep me grounded to the earth but would be good protection from the bugs and insects? Temperature is not a huge concern because the coldest it usually gets here is around 10 F in the peak of winter and I can always add blankets if needed. Rain is another big factor because we get a shit ton of rain at times.

2) Would this be better done on grass or dirt or does it not matter?

3) For the actual structure I'm debating between a yurt or tipi at the moment, but open to other suggestions. I suspect it will only be me out here for the time being.

### Anthony Smith — 2025-05-04T06:58:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/building-an-outdoor-bed.30826/post-351872>

Are you sleeping directly on the ground or some kind of pad?

### Mr.AJ — 2025-05-04T15:51:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/building-an-outdoor-bed.30826/post-351885>

I would prefer a pad but I'm looking for a material I can sleep on that is still grounded to avoid the bugs/ticks. Something like animal hide.

### Adamo — 2025-05-16T15:57:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cuttlefish-squid-ink.30827/post-352614>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=352606)
>
> Its in oysters, other seafood, nuts, mushrooms, seeds, meats .... in the context of food and terrain of the gut, and the high turnover rate of enterocytes etc I think it would be different to say melanin extracted from something and used pharmacologically or as a supplement.

As much as eating this may be enjoyable, Uncle Jack has reiterated in several postings that the melanin found in 'foods' that humans may consume (certain mushrooms such as Chaga, squid ink, etc.) is doing what it does in humans... it sequesters the toxic material and helps to eliminate it from the body. Consuming it may not be the wisest of moves because we are all trying to create balance and increase our melanin levels, as to "detox" among many other things; not to add toxins into the system, especially if you are already dealing with other issues.

Also remember that the ink used by cuttle-fish and squid is used as a type of "weapon" to protect themselves in stressful situations or when facing something they are prey-to.

I am guilty of eating certain Italian dishes with this ink, but the logic stands. And in most circumstances, people living in the coastal regions often remove the ink-sacks from the aquatic life when preparing it for consumption.

Dr Kruse you posted #47 on Patreon tonight. Who wrote the poem called "A Poem for the Sovereign Soul" I would like permission to make a song out of some of it.

### Wanttoknow — 2025-05-04T15:23:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/47-on-patreon-who-wrote-the-poem.30828/post-351881>

Dr Kruse would you care if I made a song out of it. All movements need a battle cry.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-04T15:24:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/47-on-patreon-who-wrote-the-poem.30828/post-351883>
